votes.reason: The Python code correctly generates the list of all prefixes using list comprehension and string slicing, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly generates all prefixes of the string using slicing and compares the result to the expected list, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly computes the list of all prefixes using list comprehension and slicing, which matches the Coq specification using map, substring, and seq.

Both implementations generate all prefixes of string s from length 1 to length n. The Coq version uses `substring 0 (S i) s` for i in `seq 0 (String.length s)`, which produces substrings of lengths 1, 2, ..., n. The Python version uses `s[:i+1]` for i in `range(len(s))`, which also produces substrings of lengths 1, 2, ..., n. Both return the same list of prefixes for any concrete string input.

Both implementations generate the same list of prefixes. The Coq version uses `substring 0 (S i) s` for `i` in `seq 0 (String.length s)`, which produces substrings of lengths 1, 2, ..., len(s). The Python version uses `s[:i+1]` for `i` in `range(len(s))`, which also produces substrings of lengths 1, 2, ..., len(s). Both return the same list of prefixes for any concrete string input.

Both implementations generate all prefixes of string s from length 1 to length n. The Coq version uses substring 0 (S i) for i in [0..len-1], giving substrings of lengths 1,2,...,n. The Python version uses s[:i+1] for i in range(len(s)), which is equivalent: for i=0,1,...,n-1, it produces s[:1], s[:2], ..., s[:n]. Both return the same list of prefixes for any concrete string input.
Both produce the list of all non-empty prefixes of s (lengths 1..len(s)), and return [] for the empty string. Index ranges and slicing/substring semantics align.
Both produce the list of prefixes of s of lengths 1..len(s), matching for all concrete strings including the empty string.
Both produce the list of non-empty prefixes of s, from length 1 to len(s), in order; for empty s both return [].
